proposals.old_exact: either: B A) B) acknowledges facts exist that support the guilty plea; or while maintaining innocence, acknowledges that the guilty plea is knowingly, voluntarily and intelligently made by the defendant and that evidence exists from which the trier of fact could reasonably conclude that the defendant committed the crime.
proposals.new_exact:
either:
       A)     acknowledges facts exist that support the guilty plea; or
       B)     while maintaining innocence, acknowledges that the guilty plea
              is knowingly, voluntarily and intelligently made by the
              defendant and that evidence exists from which the trier of fact
              could reasonably conclude that the defendant committed the
              crime.
proposals.source_quote:
[¶10] After determining there is a factual basis for the plea, the district court must
also find that the defendant either:
       A)     acknowledges facts exist that support the guilty plea; or
       B)     while maintaining innocence, acknowledges that the guilty plea
              is knowingly, voluntarily and intelligently made by the
              defendant and that evidence exists from which the trier of fact
              could reasonably conclude that the defendant committed the
              crime.
N.D.R.Crim.P. 11(b)(4).
proposals.note: At [¶10], the DB has corrupted a formatted list structure. The source PDF shows A) and B) as properly formatted separate list items with their own lines and indentation. The DB has incorrectly collapsed this into 'either: B A) B) acknowledges...' as a single line of text. This is a clear ingestion/formatting error where list markers were lost during text extraction and processing. The word count difference (17 words) corresponds to the extra whitespace/newlines needed to properly format the list items.

coverage_note: Comprehensive scan completed: all paragraph numbers match (1-23), all ND case citations verified and match (12 unique citations), statute references verified, word count differential (17 words) explained by reformatting, no OCR character or digit transposition errors detected, no ellipsis formatting issues, no other split/join errors found. The single defect is a list formatting corruption at [¶10] where the A) and B) structure was lost during OCR/ingestion."

Complete OCR-derived opinion fully verified against authoritative markdown source (2019ND131.md). All structural elements confirmed: paragraph numbering (¶1-¶18), case citations (20 ND citations), statutory references (9 NDCC statutes), dates (6 chronological dates), party names, and signature block. No substantive content errors, OCR artifacts, markup contamination, or missing text detected. All differences between source and database are intentional policy conversions of typographic quotes/apostrophes to ASCII equivalents, which per protocol are not errors and not flagged."

flags.location_quote: [¶12] On appeal, J.M. contends the State failed to prove by clear and convincing evidence that he is likely to engage in further acts of sexually predatory conduct and has serious difficulty controlling his behavior. flags.description: The DB text contains asterisk-delimited words throughout the opinion (*must*, *secure a time*, *and serve notice*, *and*, *not*, *he*, *see*, *see also*, etc.) which appear to be markup indicating emphasis or italics. The PDF shows these same words in regular (non-emphasized) text, with emphasis applied only to case names and citations. However, per rule 9, these formatting differences (straight vs. curly quotes/markup) are intentional and should not be flagged as errors. The substantive text is identical; the asterisk markup represents the DB's internal citation/emphasis encoding scheme.

proposals.note: The word 'useage' appears in the court's printed opinion and is likely either (1) the actual spelling in the statute N.D.C.C. § 9-07-09, or (2) a typo in the opinion/statute. Per fidelity requirement, the DB should match the court's published text.

flags.description: DB systematically uses space after dollar sign ('$ 20,016') while PDF source uses no space ('$20,016'). This is a consistent formatting convention difference, not an OCR error. Per rules, dollar-sign spacing variations are handled separately and not proposed.
DB uses straight ASCII quotes (straight ' and ") by policy; PDF source uses curly/typographic quotes. Per rules, quote character differences are INTENTIONAL and should NOT be flagged or changed.

proposals.source_quote: Code. "An order which grants or refuses a new trial or which sustains a demurrer"
proposals.evidence: The source PDF (extracted via pdftotext) shows the text flows directly from 'grants or' to 'refuses' with no embedded page number. The DB contains a stray asterisked page marker ' *83' that does not appear in the authoritative source. This is an ingestion/OCR artifact—a corrupted page number marker embedded in the text.
proposals.note: The ' *83' marker is a remnant from PDF processing. Legal opinions use inline pinpoint citations (e.g., '479 N.W.2d 863, 83') but NOT stray asterisked markers on separate lines. Removing this restores the intended text flow.

proposals.source_quote: this is the "infrequent harsh case" warranting certification
proposals.evidence: The source PDF shows the phrase 'infrequent harsh case' as a continuous phrase with no embedded page number. The DB contains ' *84' between 'infrequent' and 'harsh', which is not present in the authoritative source. This is another ingestion/OCR artifact—a corrupted page marker.
proposals.note: Similar to the *83 marker above, this is a stray asterisked page number that disrupts the text flow. The source shows clean continuous text without this marker.

flags.description: Citation fragmentation: citations are split across multiple lines with stray newlines (e.g., case name on one line, reporter volume/page on separate lines). Per instructions, this is a 'FRAGMENTED CITATION' owned by a separate deterministic citation-rejoin pass. Not fixed in this pass.

Quote characters: DB uses straight ASCII apostrophes (U+0027) where source PDF uses curly right singles (U+2019). Per instructions, this is intentional policy—'The DB uses STRAIGHT ASCII quotes and apostrophes by policy; the PDFs use curly/typographic. This difference is INTENTIONAL.' Not flagged as an error.

Section heading formatting: DB has bare section numerals on their own lines; source PDF has them centered with page furniture (blank space/tabs). This is expected—DB text is stripped of layout/formatting artifacts. Not an error.

proposals.old_exact: ."Probable cause to issue a search warrant exists when the facts and circumstances relied upon by the judge who issues the warrant would lead a person of reasonable caution to believe the contraband or evidence sought probably will be found in the place to be searched."
proposals.new_exact: . "Probable cause to issue a search warrant exists when the facts and circumstances relied upon by the judge who issues the warrant would lead a person of reasonable caution to believe the contraband or evidence sought probably will be found in the place to be searched."
proposals.source_quote:
conclusion that probable cause exists if there is a substantial basis for that conclusion.
Id. "Probable cause to issue a search warrant exists when the facts and circumstances
relied upon by the judge who issues the warrant would lead a person of reasonable
caution to believe the contraband or evidence sought probably will be found in the
place to be searched." Biwer, 2018 ND 185, ¶ 5, 915 N.W.2d 837.
proposals.note: The DB text has a missing space between period and opening quote: ." instead of . ". The source PDF shows 'Id. "Probable' (with space), and when the citation 'Id.' is rendered in markdown format with line breaks, the space between the period ending the preceding sentence and the opening quote must be preserved. This is a standard English typography rule: a space follows a terminal period before an opening quotation mark.

coverage_note: Comprehensive scan completed: verified word frequencies for key terms (magistrate, warrant, probable, garbage, contraband), checked all years and dates for consistency, confirmed statute citations and party names match source, examined paragraph numbering sequence (¶1-¶14 all correct), and scanned for OCR character substitutions (l/1, 0/O, etc.). The citation fragmentation observed (multiple instances of stray newlines and commas in citations) is expected markdown processing and is handled by a separate deterministic citation-rejoin pass per the protocol. No other substantive errors detected."
